title: Old Marrow, the Ferryman
statblock: No combat block. If the party attacks him, he does not defend: he steps off the ferry and walks into the river, gone beneath the surface. The ferry drifts to the far bank on its own, party aboard. He is at his post the next morning. Use **Commoner** if a mechanical reference is required; he will not use it.
special_element: Old Marrow is older than the river. He was here when this valley was dry land, ferrying people across something else the locals have long since stopped remembering. The water came later; he stayed. The coins he drops over the rail are not payment — they are the old way of sealing a crossing-contract with the dry earth beneath, a habit he has never broken. Not undead, not fey, not a god: simply very old and very patient, and whatever bound him to this crossing has not yet let him go.
summary: Old Marrow keeps the ferry, and Old Marrow does not blink.
body: Old Marrow keeps the ferry, and Old Marrow does not blink. He is thin as a peeled branch, in a coat the colour of river silt, and when he turns to greet you he already knows your name — not *a* name, yours, the one your mother used. His voice is a rope dragged over wet wood. Every coin he takes from your palm goes over the rail into the river. Never a purse. Never a pocket.

Old Marrow has worked this crossing since before anyone can say otherwise. He knows every passenger by name before they speak and every destination before they ask, and he explains neither. He offers no pleasantries beyond what it takes to get the party aboard and across.
He is not threatening. He is not exactly welcoming. He is simply *there*, the way the bank is there — as though the crossing would not function without him.

He answers questions about the river, the far bank, and the road ahead in short, accurate words. He will not answer questions about himself, except one: *"How long have you kept this ferry?"* To that he says, without pausing the oar: **"Since before the water was here."**
He is telling the truth.

Special element: Old Marrow is older than the river. He was here when this valley was dry land, ferrying people across something else the locals have long since stopped remembering. The water came later; he stayed. The coins he drops over the rail are not payment — they are the old way of sealing a crossing-contract with the dry earth beneath, a habit he has never broken. Not undead, not fey, not a god: simply very old and very patient, and whatever bound him to this crossing has not yet let him go.
dm_tips: **Introduce:** The ferry is already at the bank when the party arrives, as though it was waiting. Old Marrow is at the oar without having been seen to approach it.

**Voice/play:** Never blink. Speak slowly, with long pauses before answers — not as if thinking, but as if remembering from very far back. Use each character's real name the first time he addresses them, unprompted, before anyone has introduced themselves.
**Wants:** Nothing urgent. He wants the crossing made, the coin in the river, and the passengers off on the far side. He is not collecting debts. He is not watching. He completes crossings.

**Reveal:** If a player asks how he knows their name: *"I know everyone who crosses. I have known you since before you had a name for it."* Save the *"since before the water was here"* line for when someone presses his age — it lands hardest after he's already unsettled them once. The full truth (he predates the river; the coins are a land-contract ritual, not a toll) is never stated aloud. Let the party piece it together, or not.
**Dialogue seeds:** *"You're earlier than I expected."* / *"The far bank is dry today. It will not always be."* / *"Everyone crosses eventually."*

title: The Saltwidow
statblock:
```
STAT BLOCK — The Saltwidow                        (5e-compatible)
AC 13 (natural resilience) · HP 84 (13d8+26) · Speed 30 ft
STR 10  DEX 13  CON 14  INT 18  WIS 20  CHA 17
Saves: Wis +9, Cha +7
Skills: Insight +9, Perception +9, History +8, Persuasion +7
Condition Immunities: charmed, frightened
Senses: truesight 30 ft · Passive Perception 19

Key action: TAKE THE MEMORY (Recharge 5–6). One creature within 30 ft makes a
DC 17 Wisdom save or loses a memory (DM's choice). On a fail: 4d8 psychic
damage, disadvantage on Insight checks until a long rest. The Saltwidow regains
HP equal to half the psychic damage dealt.

Spells (Warlock 13, coast-patron flavour): Eldritch Blast (grey salt-spray),
Misty Step, Detect Thoughts, Modify Memory, Legend Lore.

CR 9  (scale: easy = CR 6, hard = CR 12 — see Difficulty)
```

She will not initiate combat. If the party steals the locket and runs, she does not pursue at once — she sends something, and they will understand at once that she could have done worse.
special_element: A borrowed memory keeps the Saltwidow from aging — a decade or two each, before it fades — and she has strung three centuries together this way, outliving everyone she ever dealt with. The one memory she believes will hold for good is a child's last unclouded summer: the exact texture of a life in the weeks before it broke. She cannot make it and cannot take it from someone who never carried it. She has felt it twice in three hundred years, both times in people she could not reach a deal with before they were gone. She knows it is out in the world right now. She can feel it the moment it enters a room.
summary: Category: NPC · Tier: 3 · Biome: coast · Role: informant · Mood: grim · Difficulty: hard
body: Category: NPC · Tier: 3 · Biome: coast · Role: informant · Mood: grim · Difficulty: hard

She is already seated when you arrive, back to the door, facing the water. The first thing you notice is the smell — not salt, not rot, but something inland and weeks gone: cut grass, warm stone, a summer that ended somewhere else. When she turns, she does not look at the group. She looks at each of you in turn, once, the way a person counts coins. Her eyes are the colour of someone else's, and they change as they move across you.

The Saltwidow has kept this stretch of coast as long as anyone can remember — which is, she would tell you, the problem. She trades favours for memories. A secret you need, a name that opens a locked door, safe passage through a guarded shore: the price is always a memory. A small one, she says. Nothing you will miss.

She is not cruel about it. She is precise. She asks questions that seem personal and incidental until you realise she is gauging what you can afford to lose — *tell me about the house you grew up in; tell me about the first time you were afraid* — and she takes the smallest viable piece. She does not take what you will mourn.

She holds the memories rather than spends them, and while she holds one she does not age. But they dim — within a decade or two each goes pale, then dark — and when one goes, the years it kept at bay arrive all at once. She has managed the trade for three hundred years.
There is one memory she has been hunting that whole time. She has come close twice. She keeps an empty locket for the day she gets it.

Special element: A borrowed memory keeps the Saltwidow from aging — a decade or two each, before it fades — and she has strung three centuries together this way, outliving everyone she ever dealt with. The one memory she believes will hold for good is a child's last unclouded summer: the exact texture of a life in the weeks before it broke. She cannot make it and cannot take it from someone who never carried it. She has felt it twice in three hundred years, both times in people she could not reach a deal with before they were gone. She knows it is out in the world right now. She can feel it the moment it enters a room.
dm_tips: **Introduce:** She is already there when the party arrives, seated, facing away, as though she expected them. She does not stand.

**Voice/play:** Quiet, unhurried, always a beat behind where the conversation expects her — she answers the question before the one you just asked. Her eyes shift colour when her attention moves. Do not name the shift; let the players catch it themselves.

**Wants:** The memory the empty locket is waiting for. She will give almost anything for it — information, a lifted curse, a name no one living should know — and will not say why until pressed hard. Even then, only: *"It is the one I have been looking for."*

**Reveal:** If a PC carries the missing childhood summer (via the character hook), she knows it the moment they walk in. She has been waiting. Hold the reveal until a player asks why she keeps looking at one of them — then answer flat, no drama: *"Because I have something of yours. And I think you have come to take it back."*
**Dialogue seeds:**
*"Tell me about the summer you were eight. Not what happened. How the light looked."*
*"I am not what I was. No one is. I simply remember the distance."*
*"The memory in the locket is not mine. I have been keeping it safe. That is what I tell myself."*

title: Ser Aldous the Unfallen
statblock:
```
STAT BLOCK — Ser Aldous the Unfallen               (5e-compatible)
AC 18 (battle-scarred full plate) · HP 210 (20d10+100) · Speed 30 ft
STR 20 (+5)  DEX 10 (+0)  CON 20 (+5)  INT 14 (+2)  WIS 18 (+4)  CHA 16 (+3)
Saving Throws: Str +10, Con +10, Wis +9, Cha +8
Skills: Athletics +10, History +7, Perception +9, Religion +7
Damage Immunities: necrotic, poison
Condition Immunities: charmed, exhaustion, frightened, poisoned
Senses: Darkvision 60 ft · Passive Perception 19
Languages: Common, Celestial
CR 12  (scale: tune the enemy threat, not Aldous — he fights at full strength each time)

Key action: The Fifth Return (Recharge 6). When reduced to 0 HP, Ser Aldous
stabilises at 1 HP; his weapon attacks deal an additional 2d10 necrotic damage
until the end of combat. This trait cannot trigger a fifth time — on the fourth
use, he stays down. Track uses openly at the table.
```
special_element: Ser Aldous carries a list. He started it after the second death and added to it each time — four entries in careful soldier's script: *laughter — dreams — heartbeat — the name she called me.* He will show it to the party if they ask what is different about him. The fifth line is blank. He knows it will not stay blank.
summary: Category: NPC · Tier: 4 · Biome: ruins · Mood: heroic · Role: ally · Difficulty: hard
body: Category: NPC · Tier: 4 · Biome: ruins · Mood: heroic · Role: ally · Difficulty: hard

He stands in the rubble like something that forgot to fall. The armour has been mended four times in four different metals — iron, bronze, a dull grey alloy no one makes anymore, and at the shoulder, something that looks almost like bone. When he speaks, his voice arrives a half-beat before and after itself, as though two Aldouses are standing just out of step. His shadow falls wrong: not absent, but *early* — already pointing somewhere he hasn't moved yet.

Ser Aldous was a paladin of the old order. He died at the Siege of Carrenmere and came back. He died defending a village he can no longer name and came back. Then twice more, and each return took something with it: first his laughter, then his dreams, then the sound of his own heartbeat — he checks for it now by pressing two fingers to his throat. The fourth death took the name his mother called him. He can recite his title, his oaths, his tally. He cannot tell you what she called him.
He is not mad. He is not undead. He is simply less than he was, in exact measured increments, and he knows every subtraction by number.

He has come to the ruins — and to the party — because he is walking toward the one place where his fifth death can mean something. He wants help reaching the thing that keeps returning him, and he wants them to make sure that this time it ends. He asks plainly. He is not dramatic about it.

Special element: Ser Aldous carries a list. He started it after the second death and added to it each time — four entries in careful soldier's script: *laughter — dreams — heartbeat — the name she called me.* He will show it to the party if they ask what is different about him. The fifth line is blank. He knows it will not stay blank.
dm_tips: **Introduce:** He is already there when the party arrives — sitting in the rubble, no fire, no supplies, no hurry. He rises when he sees them with the slow steadiness of a man who has practised getting up from worse.

**Voice/play:** Calm, measured, very slightly doubled, as though hearing himself on a short delay. He pauses a half-beat too long before nouns, retrieving them from somewhere further away each time. When he says something that would once have been funny, he does not smile. Laughter is on the list.
**Wants:** Help reaching the thing that keeps returning him, and a guarantee that when it is done, it is done for both of them. He asks directly. He does not beg or bargain.

**Reveal:** Show the list at a quiet moment, never mid-combat. Set it on the ground between them and let the party read it. Say nothing until someone asks about the blank line. *"I don't know what goes there. I have wondered whether I'll have enough left to notice."*
**Dialogue seeds:** *"I have died four times. The fifth is not a fear. It is a timetable."* / *"When you see it, you will want to retreat. Whatever I do next — don't."*

title: The Hollow Cantor
statblock:
```
STAT BLOCK — The Hollow Cantor               (5e-compatible)
AC 12 · HP 82 (11d8+33) · Speed 30 ft
STR 9   DEX 12  CON 16  INT 14  WIS 20  CHA 18
Skills: Insight +9, Persuasion +8, Religion +6
Damage Immunities: necrotic
Condition Immunities: frightened, charmed

Dirge of Unrest (sustained, no action required). While the Cantor sings,
each living creature within 60 ft that can hear him loses 3 (1d6) HP at the
start of each of its turns (Con save DC 17 halves). A creature reduced to
0 HP this way rises the following round as a Shadow bound to the Cantor's will.

Key action: Keen the Dead — the Cantor raises his voice. Up to three Shadows
within 120 ft immediately make one attack each against a target of his choice.

CR 9 · real encounter difficulty scales with accumulated Shadows
(easy = Cantor alone, CR5 effective; hard = Cantor + 4–6 Shadows, CR 12 effective)
```
special_element: He is overjoyed to see you. He has not spoken to the living in decades, and his happiness is genuine and unguarded — he will offer food (he has none) and ask after the world above with the eagerness of a man who has simply been away too long. He does not know he is killing you. This is the thing the DM must hold: there is no malice here, none at all. There is only the song, still going, drawing warmth from whatever is nearest. The horror is that stopping him is the right call — and he will not understand why you must.
summary: Category: NPC · Region: — · Role: villain · Difficulty: hard · Tier: 3 · Biome: cavern · Mood: melancholy
body: Category: NPC · Region: — · Role: villain · Difficulty: hard · Tier: 3 · Biome: cavern · Mood: melancholy

The singing reaches you before the light does — low, resonant, one voice filling the cavern the way water fills a vessel. He sits cross-legged at the cave's heart, an old man in robes the colour of bone, eyes open and fixed on a point somewhere past the ceiling. The air near him is colder than the passage you came from. The candles at his feet burn pale, almost colourless, and do not gutter — not even when you walk past them.

He was a priest of a burial order whose name nobody remembers. Forty years ago he descended into these caverns to guide the dead of a collapsed mining settlement to rest. The work, he will tell you with quiet satisfaction, went well. He has been here ever since.

The dead do find rest near the Cantor — that part is true. His voice is a genuine mercy for the departed, and the caves around him hold no restless spirits, no wailing, no clawing at the walls. The silence of the deep here is a different kind of silence from the horror-silence elsewhere in the dark. It is calm.

What he does not know — what he has never known, having been alone — is that the warmth the dead need to pass on must come from somewhere. When he sang to the miners, he drew it from the stone. After forty years, the stone has nothing left. Now the warmth comes from the nearest living thing. He will not notice your colour fading. He is already watching something else.

Special element: He is overjoyed to see you. He has not spoken to the living in decades, and his happiness is genuine and unguarded — he will offer food (he has none) and ask after the world above with the eagerness of a man who has simply been away too long. He does not know he is killing you. This is the thing the DM must hold: there is no malice here, none at all. There is only the song, still going, drawing warmth from whatever is nearest. The horror is that stopping him is the right call — and he will not understand why you must.
dm_tips: **Introduce:** The song first, then the light, then him. Let the party find him still singing before he notices them. He stops mid-phrase, looks up, and smiles with the relief of someone who has been waiting. "Oh," he says. "You are *warm*."

**Voice/play:** Soft, unhurried, slightly out of time with the world. He pauses mid-sentence not for breath but because the song runs beneath his speech and occasionally surfaces — he hums a bar without noticing. His warmth is entirely genuine. There is nothing sinister in his manner, ever.
**Wants:** To finish the song properly, to be of service, to hear news of the world above. He will ask the party to stay. He will ask them to let him help.

**Reveal:** When a party member mentions a comrade lost in these caves, the Cantor nods with deep satisfaction. "Yes, I have them. Resting, now. You can feel it — that quietness." Spring the full truth only after a successful DC 18 Arcana or Religion check reveals why the party feels cold and tired: the warmth is going somewhere. Then let him, carefully and not unkindly, explain what he does — still unaware that what he calls *passing on* is what they might call *taking*.

**Dialogue seeds:** *"The dead are frightened, mostly. It helps, the singing. It always has."* / *"I thought I would only be a season. The work kept finding me."* / *"Oh, don't go yet. I have not heard a living voice in — well. I lose track, in the deep."*
